career.notes: Lake Washington School District currently has no specific English/History openings posted but accepts general applications for future positions. Northshore, Marysville, and other districts in the region primarily use pool postings or general applications for 2026-27 anticipated vacancies. Most districts hire heavily April-June and again in August for the upcoming school year.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded 2025-26 auditions, but email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to be added to the August 2026 list for all voice parts. Rehearsals: Tuesdays 6:30-9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave). Dues $75/yr, COVID vaccination required. Seattle Choral Company accepts rolling applications; submit the online form for a 30-minute slot with Artistic Director Freddie Coleman. Prepare two unaccompanied choral excerpts, demonstrate range, intervals, and read Latin/French/German/Italian/Spanish. ~60 singers, rehearsals Monday evenings. ChoralSounds Northwest (part of Northwest Associated Arts) is actively auditioning ages 17+ for ChoralSounds and SilverSounds; fill out the Google Form to schedule. Rehearsals: Tuesdays 7-9 pm at John Knox Presbyterian Church, Normand [TRUNCATED]
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries offer weekly Toddler Storytime at both Edmonds and Lynnwood branches. Edmonds: Mondays 9:30 & 10:30 am (doors unlock 9:15-9:35); Lynnwood: Mondays 9:30 am with Nathalie. Both feature stories, songs, movement, and post-story play or art for ages 18-36 months. Imagine Children’s Museum (Everett) is ideal for 2-year-olds—three floors of hands-on play including a forest trail, wildlife rescue, and water tables. FREE Community Access Night is April 16; regular admission $14 adults/$12 kids 1-17. Check the museum calendar for WonderFest (interactive story time, dragon crafts) and Sensory Time (3rd Sundays, autism-friendly).

global_news.findings: Suspect charged with attempted assassination of Trump at Washington dinner. Cole Tomas Allen, 31, from California, has been charged with attempting to assassinate President Trump during the White House Correspondents' Dinner. The suspect allegedly wanted to kill as many high-level officials as possible.

Germany commits to major NATO military expansion. German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier announced Germany will step up its military role in NATO amid increased uncertainty, pledging 500 billion euros ($569 billion) for defense infrastructure as Europe faces what he called 'epochal geopolitical shifts' with Putin's war in Ukraine.

Ceasefire at risk as Pakistan and Afghanistan report cross-border attacks. Tensions are escalating between Pakistan and Afghanistan with reports of cross-border attacks threatening the fragile ceasefire agreement between the two nations.

UK to stop foreign sex offenders receiving refugee protections. Britain will exclude foreign sex offenders from asylum protections as part of new border security measures. The government's Border Security, Asylum and Immigration Bill will be amended to deny refugee status to any foreign national with criminal convictions qualifying them for the sex offenders register.

King Charles III begins US state visit amid British tensions. King Charles and Queen Camilla arrived in Washington for a four-day state visit, where the King will address Congress and express sympathy over the recent assassination attempt on President Trump. The visit comes at a time of strained UK-US relations.

intellectual_journals.findings: Ian Penman explores the Beach Boys' legacy as a form of secular gospel, arguing that Brian Wilson's 1965-66 compositions ('Don't Talk', 'Caroline, No', 'I Just Wasn't Made for These Times') achieve a cathedral-like transcendence. Penman positions the band at the intersection of Saturday-night carnality and Sunday-morning sacredness, noting how their music navigates the tension between rock's origins and its rare intimations of the divine. The essay traces Wilson's evolution from novelty pop to creating what Penman calls 'secular hymns baited with pop hooks,' while acknowledging the darker narr [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy examines literature's role in personal freedom through Proust's disagreement with Ruskin's moralizing defense of reading. Proust argued that books aren't instruments of moral betterment but preserve indelible impressions that make time tangible—something to be grasped without being abolished. The essay positions reading as a unique form of communication that connects us with powerful minds, distinct from social life's constrained speech. Champy explores how literature operates outside cost-benefit mindsets, offering not instruction but the preservation of past sensations that shap [TRUNCATED]

Will Alden reports on Iranian opposition exiles in Los Angeles who gathered for an Easter Sunday picnic organized by the Constitutionalist Party of Iran (CPI). The event reveals the complex diaspora politics surrounding Reza Pahlavi's campaign to position himself as a 'transitional leader' for Iran amid US-Israel military operations. Alden documents how CPI's weekly protests at Westwood's Wilshire Federal Building have drawn thousands, creating a significant Republican constituency while critics argue war remains the only urgent action against the Islamic Republic. The piece captures the surre [TRUNCATED]

A joint ProPublica-FRONTLINE investigation reveals that over 300 protesters arrested during Trump administration immigration sweeps saw their cases collapse under scrutiny. Federal agents routinely accused dissenters of being domestic terrorists, but more than a third of cases were dismissed when video footage debunked officer statements. The investigation documents how mass arrests—including a Marine veteran arrested for distributing water to protesters—created a chilling effect on dissent even when charges failed. The piece exposes how conspiracy statutes typically used against drug traffick [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova explores the construction of selfhood as a selective retelling of cosmic chance events—from atomic bonding to parental pairings to cultural values. Drawing on Iris Murdoch's observation that 'the self is a place of illusion,' the essay positions the dissolution of selfish consciousness as the great achievement of existence. Popova weaves Blaise Pascal's terror at the arbitrariness of existence with Philip Larkin's assertion that 'what will survive of us is love,' arguing that love is how we survive the cosmic helplessness of being born ourselves. The piece frames self-questioning  [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Three 2025-2026 papers advance triadic ontologies: a physics-rooted thesis argues reality emerges via simultaneous closure of Binding, Transformation & Relational Readout roles; a quantum-ontology article builds an SU(3)-invariant qutrit kernel with triadic states, operators & constraints; and a neurophenomenology paper unifies material, mental & axiological domains through consciousness, offering testable neural signatures and AI relevance.

ai_systems.findings: 2026 is the year multi-agent AI systems moved from research curiosity to enterprise backbone. Google Cloud's new PaperOrchestra framework shows the state-of-the-art: five specialized agents collaborate to turn raw lab notes into submission-ready LaTeX manuscripts, making ~6,000 LLM calls per paper with API-verified citations and auto-generated figures. Meanwhile, IBM reports that enterprises using multi-agent architectures now achieve significantly higher task-completion rates than single-agent setups, with visual no-code builders like DIMA-AI letting non-technical teams orchestrate complex workflows on kanban boards. The architectural consensus is crystallizing around an orchestrator pattern: a meta-agent farms subtasks to specialist agents, then synthesizes the results, which is why even astrophysics teams are winning competitions with semi-autonomous agent swarms like Cmbagent. Prompt engineering has flipped: 2023-era chain-of-thought hand-holding now backfires on reasoning models ( [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congress is accelerating its UAP-disclosure drive. On April 1 the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, chaired by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, formally demanded the Pentagon hand over 46 classified UAP videos and all supporting data by April 14; the deadline was missed and the stand-off continues. A March 25 classified briefing inside a Capitol SCIF—first confirmed by Rep. Eric Burlison—has become the center of a widening mystery after retired Air Force Maj. Gen. Neil McCasland, former commander of the Air Force Research Laboratory at Wright-Patterson and a key aerospace witness, vanished from his Albuquerque home on February 27. His disappearance, along with those of several other scientists tied to advanced propulsion programs, was discussed inside the SCIF, according to members present. President Trump, responding to public pressure and a viral remark by former President Obama, issued a February 20 directive ordering Defense Secretary Hegseth and the All-Domain Anomaly Resolut [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The Return of the Native
newyorker.dek: A celebrated novelist comes home to Brooklyn—and finds that the borough has changed more than he has.
newyorker.text:
On a recent Tuesday afternoon, the novelist Jonathan Lethem stood on the stoop of a brownstone in Boerum Hill, squinting up at the building he’d once called home. Twenty-five years ago, Lethem immortalized this stretch of Bergen Street in his breakout novel, “Motherless Brooklyn,” turning the quiet block into a literary landmark. Now, he was back—not as a character, but as a visitor.

The neighborhood, he noted, had become almost unrecognizable. The bodega where his protagonist once bought loosies had been replaced by a minimalist café serving twenty-two-dollar grain bowls. The check-cashing place was now a boutique selling “curated vintage.” Even the street art had changed: gone were the chaotic tags of the ’90s, replaced by commissioned murals with corporate sponsors.

“I feel like I’m walking through a memory that’s been Photoshopped,” Lethem said, half-laughing. He wore a faded denim jacket and carried a canvas tote bag—Brooklyn uniform, circa 2006. A woman in Lululemon power-walked past, AirPods in, and didn’t glance up.

Inside the brownstone, the current owner, a tech executive named Priya, greeted him warmly. She’d read “Motherless Brooklyn” in college and had bought the place partly because of it. “It’s like living inside a novel,” she said, leading him through the renovated kitchen (Sub-Zero fridge, brass fixtures) and up to the roof deck with its Manhattan skyline view.

Lethem paused at the top of the stairs. “This used to be a crawl space,” he said. “We kept old paint cans here.” Now, there were string lights and a succulent garden. He ran his hand along the exposed brick, as if checking for something lost.

Downstairs, Priya’s husband, a venture capitalist, offered him a matcha. “We love the literary history,” he said. “It’s part of the home’s brand.” Lethem nodded politely, but his eyes kept drifting to the backyard, where a child’s playhouse—miniature modernist, all cedar and glass—had replaced the weed-choked lot where he once wrote longhand drafts.

As he left, Lethem lingered on the stoop. A delivery guy on an e-bike zipped by, dropping off groceries for the neighbors. Somewhere, a smart speaker played Fleet Foxes. “It’s not worse,” he said finally. “Just—different. Like the borough got gentrified by my own nostalgia.”

He walked toward Smith Street, past a new bookstore (independent, but with a wine bar) and a yoga studio offering “mindful resistance training.” At the corner, he stopped to watch a film crew setting up for a Netflix series. The director, a woman in a Patagonia vest, waved him over. “We’re shooting a scene set in 1999,” she said. “Do you mind if we use you as background? You’ve got the right look.”

Lethem laughed—loudly, genuinely—and shook his head. “I’m just a ghost,” he said, and kept walking.
